text.title: Go Concurrency Antipatterns
text.summary: Goroutine leak, data race, WaitGroup misuse, and channel deadlock — with concrete bad/good pairs.

antipattern.description: Generator goroutine without a ctx.Done() exit: go func() { for i := 0; ; i++ { ch <- i } }()
antipattern.reason: If no consumer reads from ch, the goroutine blocks forever. Memory and goroutine count grow unboundedly.

antipattern.description: Concurrent write to a plain map without a mutex: go func() { cache[key]++ }()
antipattern.reason: Concurrent map access is undefined behaviour in Go. The race detector catches it, but it may silently corrupt data or panic in production.

antipattern.description: Calling wg.Add(1) inside the goroutine body: go func() { wg.Add(1); defer wg.Done(); ... }()
antipattern.reason: The goroutine may not execute before wg.Wait() is called if the scheduler yields, causing premature completion.

rule.statement: Always call wg.Add(1) before launching the goroutine, never inside it.

section.title: Race Detector Requirement

rule.statement: All tests for concurrency-heavy packages must pass go test -race -count=10 ./... before merge. Never skip the race detector.
rule.rationale: Concurrency bugs look correct under low load. The race detector and repeated runs surface them reliably.

section.reference: CI script enforcing race detector and staticcheck
